name: BloodHound MCP AI
what_it_does: An MCP server that connects BloodHound's Active Directory attack path analysis to AI assistants, enabling security professionals to query AD privilege escalation paths, Kerberos vulnerabilities, and certificate service issues using natural language instead of Cypher queries.
use_cases: Querying BloodHound AD attack path data in natural language to identify privilege escalation routes
Automating security assessment reporting by asking an AI to analyze kerberoastable users or NTLM relay vulnerabilities
Accelerating red team operations by letting analysts ask complex graph traversal questions without writing Cypher
not_for: Unauthorized security assessments — requires explicit permission for target environments
Environments without BloodHound 4.x+ and Neo4j already set up with AD data loaded
Blue team / defensive use cases without pre-existing BloodHound infrastructure
best_when: A red team or security analyst has BloodHound with AD data loaded and wants to accelerate attack path discovery through natural language queries.
avoid_when: You do not already have BloodHound infrastructure in place, or you are conducting unauthorized testing.

auth.notes: Connects to Neo4j via environment variables (URI, username, password). No additional auth layer on the MCP server itself — relies on Neo4j access control.

pricing.notes: Community project, no license explicitly stated in summary. BloodHound CE (prerequisite) is Apache 2.0.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Community project with limited documentation on error handling; Cypher query failures may produce raw Neo4j errors.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: All 75+ tools are read-only graph queries — fully idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: No MCP server-level authentication — any process with MCP access can query your AD data
Requires BloodHound 4.x+ with AD data already ingested — not plug-and-play
Community project (337 stars) with no dedicated maintainer SLA; security patches may be slow
Natural language to Cypher translation quality depends on the underlying LLM — hallucinated queries may return empty or misleading results
